text:
Interviewer: Yes, but it's really fun to talk about this particular topic because our recruitment process has, I'd say since August, changed drastically. Normally we work continuously trying to fill our network with interesting people to grow with and we keep individuals warm, and then we move into a very structured recruitment process with interviews and screening. So it's one thing and another before we actually decide that we're in a phase where we want to hire. That's not how it looks at all right now. It's very difficult at the moment among our customers, and I want to emphasize that we're a consulting firm. So it's not that we hire for our own product, but we hire based on the needs that exist in the market with our customers. It's harder than ever to predict what vision our customers have. It really varies between many different types of roles and the same thing in terms of what types of skills they're looking for. So ideally, as a company we want to look at where we are right now and then set a future state and recruit based on the vision and mission that we're striving for or living up to. But because our strategy continues to be about growth and expanding, it's very difficult to simply predict what we want, what we want to be, what competencies we need further down the line. And at the same time deliver that when we have a strategy of actually wanting to grow. So, so picky, if I may put it that way, we can't afford to be right now because the market is very hard to assess. So we've completely changed our recruitment process. We constantly contact potential candidates against assignment requests that exist in the market. So we have a very large network among our customers and we get a lot of assignment requests, and as soon as we get one we map it against the candidates we've connected with. This means we no longer do the screening of these participants, these recruits ourselves. Instead we help these potential future employees develop a CV, develop a portfolio. A portfolio is almost a requirement for all assignment requests right now, I'd say. And then they go into interviews and screenings with our customers. So indirectly our customers screen our potential candidates for us. And provided they then get an assignment, that's only then that they get employment with us. So indirectly we could say we almost outsource the screening to our customers, and when an assignment is locked to a person, that's when we also tie them to an employment contract with us. Why have we done it this way? Well, as I mentioned, it's very difficult right now to assess what abilities, what competencies and what skills are in demand. That swings very, very quickly, very, very quickly in terms of what need exists out there. And we don't have the ability in this downturn that exists right now to take the risk of hiring and hoping that a consultant within x months will then get an assignment. When we've done that we see that it can sometimes take six to twelve months before the person gets an assignment, and that would just risk jeopardizing the entire company and all employees by applying our normal recruitment process right now. So we call it hiring against assignments, that is, we try to help our potential future employees get assignments, and if they get the assignment, we hire them in connection with that.
